coredata.dc:title: Identification of kynurenine pathway kynurenic acid in biological systems using nanosensor fabricated from 7-bromoquinoline-2-carboxylic acid and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Rumei
Fang, Yuying
Hu, Zhixuan
Li, Hui
Li, Juncheng
Nan, Kaihui
Zhao, Yune
Ou, Shengju
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we successfully synthesized 7-bromoquinoline-2-carboxylic acid (BA)–functionalized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (BA-NCQDs) in an aqueous medium. The as-prepared BA-NCQDs demonstrated potential in selectively detecting kynurenic acid (KYNA). Upon exposure to varying concentrations of KYNA, the fluorescence of BA-NCQDs underwent quenching, attributed to a static quenching mechanism and enhancement of the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) molecular effect, implying a complex interaction between BA-NCQDs and KYNA. Density functional theory calculations revealed robust hydrogen bonding between KYNA and the amide linker in BA-NCQDs. The limit of detection (LOD) was established at 0.05 μM. Furthermore, the BA-NCQDs nanosensor effectively imaged KYNA within living cells and biological tissues and detected it in human serum and urine samples. Experimental results indicate that the KYNA sensor, based on kynurenine pathway metabolites, holds considerable promise, offering a selective and reliable strategy for KYNA detection in real-world samples, which may facilitate the early diagnosis of various diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Kynurenine pathway metabolite
Kynurenic acid
Cell imaging
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Molecular mechanism of BA-NCQDs response to KYNA
Spiked-recovery sensing KYNA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) and (b) TEM of NCQDs and BA-NCQDs, the insert pictures of (i) and (ii) were size distributions of NCQDs and BA-NCQDs in aqueous solution, respectively; (c) XRD and (d) Raman spectra of (i) NCQDs and (ii) BA-NCQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the BA-NCQDs (5 mg/L) with various concentrations of KYNA; (b) Calibration plot of KYNA with fluorescence emission at 574 nm; (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the BA-NCQDs upon addition of various KYNA concentrations; (d) UV–Vis calibration plot of KYNA at 325 nm;(e) Plot of F 0/F against the concentration of KYNA fitted to Stern–Volmer equation with fluorescence emission at 574 nm; (f) lifetime decay of BA-NCQDs in the absence (red line) and presence (blue line) of KYNA. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Optimized structure of the BA-NCQDs/ KYNA complexes; (a) KYNA near the linker amide, (b) KYNA near the amide of the NCQDs moiety, and (c) KYNA near the bromine atom.

Confocal fluorescence images of ARPE-19 cells treated with BA-NCQD after the addition of (i) 0 μM; (ii) 26.4 μM; (iii) 79.3 μM; and (iv) 132 μM for an additional 0.5 h. (Scale: 20 μm). The (a), (b), (c) group; (d), (e), (f) group; (g), (h), (i) group; and (j), (k), (l) group corresponded to the cell’s fluorescence images at KYNA concentrations of 0, 26.4, 79.3, and 132 µM, respectively. (v) Linear correlation of fluorescence intensity of BA-NCQDs in cells treated with different concentrations of KYNA.

Schematic representation of the sensing mechanism of BA-NCQDs for the detection of KYNA. Green dots represent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs); the curved line represents 7-bromoquinoline-2-carboxylic acid (BA), and the cross shape represents kynurenic acid (KYNA); N,N'-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS).

head.ce:title: Identification of kynurenine pathway kynurenic acid in biological systems using nanosensor fabricated from 7-bromoquinoline-2-carboxylic acid and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Eye Hospital, School of Ophthalmology & Optometry, State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Visual Science, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou 325027, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Eye Hospital, School of Ophthalmology & Optometry, State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Visual Science, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou 325027, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hangzhou Femtosecond Test Co. Ltd., Zhejiang University National Park, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hangzhou Femtosecond Test Co. Ltd., Zhejiang University National Park, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we successfully synthesized 7-bromoquinoline-2-carboxylic acid (BA)–functionalized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (BA-NCQDs) in an aqueous medium. The as-prepared BA-NCQDs demonstrated potential in selectively detecting kynurenic acid (KYNA). Upon exposure to varying concentrations of KYNA, the fluorescence of BA-NCQDs underwent quenching, attributed to a static quenching mechanism and enhancement of the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) molecular effect, implying a complex interaction between BA-NCQDs and KYNA. Density functional theory calculations revealed robust hydrogen bonding between KYNA and the amide linker in BA-NCQDs. The limit of detection (LOD) was established at 0.05 μM. Furthermore, the BA-NCQDs nanosensor effectively imaged KYNA within living cells and biological tissues and detected it in human serum and urine samples. Experimental results indicate that the KYNA sensor, based on kynurenine pathway metabolites, holds considerable promise, offering a selective and reliable strategy for KYNA detection in real-world samples, which may facilitate the early diagnosis of various diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Kynurenic acid (KYNA), a tryptophan metabolite, is a well-known antagonist of excitatory amino acid receptors and a biomarker for certain neurogenerative diseases [1,2]. Abnormal KYNA levels in the human body are usually associated with neurological diseases such as schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, HIV-related illness, and tick-borne encephalitis [3–5]. Approximately 90 % of tryptophan is metabolized through the kynurenine pathway [6], making KYNA monitoring crucial in biomedical practice. Although chromatographic methods (i.e., gas chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography, and thin-layer chromatography) or tandem mass spectroscopy techniques accurately determine the concentration of KYNA in serum and urine, real-time observation of ongoing biological events is not possible using these techniques [3,7]. Additionally, detecting KYNA in tissues or cells using these methods typically requires digestion of the tissues or cells [8]. In comparison, fluorescence methods are more flexible, simple, and suitable for real-time, in situ intracellular tracking of metabolites [9,10]. To date, considerably few fluorescent sensors have been reported for the detection of the kynurenine pathway metabolites [11]. Klockow et al. demonstrated the fluorescence sensing of KYNA using a coumarin aldehyde compound at pH ∼ 1 [12]. A fluorescence quenching sensor for KYNA was fabricated using γ-globulin/gold/silver nanoclusters via a photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism [13]. The Tb(III) complex of a hydrogen-bonded organic framework was used to identify tryptophan and its metabolites; fluorescence quenching was achieved via PET by coordinating tryptophan or its metabolites with this complex [14]. However, these sensors often respond to tryptophan alongside KYNA. Highly selective fluorescent sensors for KYNA have been rarely reported. Moreover, owing to the lack of proper theoretical guidance, the design of fluorescent sensors for KYNA remains a considerable challenge.

Recent studies revealed that tryptophan–kynurenine metabolites exhibit a natural affinity for each other under specific conditions [15,16]. Their interactions, mainly controlled by electrostatic forces and hydrogen bonding, are reversible. This suggests that a KYNA-specific sensor could be developed by modifying a kynurenine-pathway metabolite. The modified metabolite would serve as a KYNA receptor or signal transductor. Interestingly, Craford et al. found that brominated tryptophan–kynurenine metabolites in swell sharks emit green-dominated fluorescence [17]. In particular, 7-bromoquinoline-2-carboxylic acid (BA), a metabolite found in swell sharks, has a structure similar to that of KYNA, differing only by the hydroxyl group of KYNA and the bromine tail of BA. These findings have inspired the development of a new fluorescent sensor for KYNA. Herein, a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor based on BA–functionalized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (BA-NCQDs) was constructed by immobilizing BA onto nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) to monitor trace amounts of KYNA.

NCQDs are a new zero-dimension carbon nanomaterial with sizes less than 10 nm [18,19]. They exhibit several outstanding properties, including high biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent water dispersibility, and cell penetration, making them suitable for various applications in bioimaging, biosensing, disease diagnosis, catalysis, and so on [20,21]. Linking NCQDs with other compounds improves both fluorescence signal transduction and target affinity [22,23] due to extension of π-conjugation. However, no studies have explored the use of NCQDs for the preparation of a KYNA sensor. In this work, BA-NCQDs nanosensor attracted KYNA resulting in formation a hydrogen bonding complex and fluorescence change. The BA-NCQDs exhibited fluorescence emission peak at 574 nm, and the fluorescence intensity evidently declined with the increasing concentrations of KYNA (Scheme 1 ). The interaction mechanism was attributed to static quenching, and the molecular mechanism was based on PET process. The as-prepared nanosensor was successfully applied to image KYNA in ARPE-19 cells and onion tissues. Its practical potential was further evaluated through spike-recovery experiments in human serum and urine, yielding satisfactory results. The nanosensor, fabricated from metabolites, offers advantages such as biocompatibility, fluorescence stability, and high signal transduction in complicated biosystems. This study provides new insights into the design and synthesis of next-generation nanosensors for amino acid metabolites.

Kynurenic acid (KYNA), 7-bromoquinoline-2-carboxylic acid (BA), 3,4,9,10-perylene tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA), N,N'-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), glycine (Gly), alanine (Ala), tryptophan (Trp), proline (Pro), lysine (Lys), isoleucine (Isoleu), methionine (Met), valine (Val), leucine (Leu), aspartate (Asp), glutamate (Glu), threonine (Thr), cysteine (Cys), arginine (Arg), histidine (His), glucose, lactose, maltose, galactose, and ribose were purchased from the Shanghai Reagent Company. Deionized water was used in all the experiments, and all other reagents were of analytical purity and used as received without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured using a PHILIPS EM400ST microscope at 150 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were acquired using an ESCALB MK-II spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected using a Spectrum GX FT-IR spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) measurements were obtained on a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 35 spectrometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were collected using a Hitachi F-4600 spectrometer. Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight (MALDI-TOF) mass spectra were obtained using an Autoflex mass spectrometer with delayed extraction and a nitrogen laser. Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) was conducted on an Agilent 6545 LC/Q-TOF.

The NCQDs were prepared following the procedure outlined in previous literature [24]. The BA-NCQDs were synthesized as follows: first, the mixture of 1 mM DCC/NHS was added to a 10 mM BA ethanol solution with stirring. After 10 min of activation, 10 mL of an NCQDs aqueous solution was added to the mixture, and the reaction was performed at room temperature for 24 h. The resulting BA-NCQDs were dialyzed against deionized water using a 500-Da dialysis bag for 72 h.

To detect the analyte, BA-NCQDs was used as the blank sample, and its fluorescence spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Subsequently, various amounts of KYNA were added to the aforementioned BA-NCQDs solution to obtain the BA-NCQDs/KYNA mixture solution. After a 5-minute incubation, the resulting mixture solution was measured, and the fluorescence spectra were measured in triplicate.

In the BA-NCQDs model, the molecular formula for NCQDs was C41H24N8O7, with its the surface featuring amide, hydroxyl, amino, and pyridine N functional groups. The BA-NCQDs is a covalent compound formed from NCQDs and BA. Interactions between BA-NCQDs and KYNA were analyzed using the DMol3 package within Materials Studio, employing density functional theory calculations. The exchange–correlation interactions used the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof generalized gradient approximation [25]. The wave function for the valence electrons was derived from the DNP basis set, which is analogous to the 6–31 G** basis set [26]. The convergence criterion for self-consistent field calculations was set at 1 × 10−6 Ha. The adsorption energy (E ad) was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
E
ad
=
E
complex
-
E
BA - N C Q D s
-
E
KYNA
,
where E complex, E BA-NCQDs, and E KYNA represent the energies of the BA-NCQDs/KYNA complex, BA-NCQDs, and KYNA, respectively.

The spike-recovery strategy was adopted in the experiments. The aim of the investigation was to verify the accuracy and stability of the detection method using the spiked recovery approach, particularly its accuracy in complex real samples. Human urine and serum were collected from the Second Affiliated Hospital of Wenzhou Medical University and ultrafiltered using a 0.25 μm-pore size filter membrane, then diluted 100-fold before use. Before measurement, the BA-NCQDs solution was incubated with different concentrations of standard analytes and real samples for 5 min. The fluorescence intensity was analyzed in triplicate.

To evaluate the cytotoxicity of BA-NCQDs against ARPE-19 cells, the CCK-8 assay was conducted after incubation periods of 24, 48, and 72 h with different concentrations of BA-NCQDs. Cytotoxicity evaluations were performed in triplicate.

Initially, ARPE-19 cells were cultured in a 24-well plate for 24 h at 37 °C. Thereafter, the cells underwent treatment with the BA-NCQDs solution for 2 h and rinsed three times with phosphate buffer saline (PBS). Subsequently, the cells were incubated with various concentrations of KYNA for an additional 0.5 h and imaged using laser confocal microscopy.

The incubation methodology for the onion tissues paralleled that for the ARPE-19 cells. To detect KYNA in onion tissues, the tissues were treated with BA-NCQDs at 37 °C for 2 h. Thereafter, they were washed three times with PBS, and diverse concentrations of KYNA were introduced to the treated groups for an additional incubation period of 0.5 h. Fluorescence images were obtained using confocal microscopy.

The TEM results and size distributions of NCQDs and BA-NCQDs are depicted in Fig. 1 a and 1b. NCQDs and BA-NCQDs exhibit a dot-like morphology and are effectively monodispersed in aqueous solutions, with average diameters of 2.99 and 3.36 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1c, the X-ray diffraction patterns of NCQDs and BA-NCQDs show analogous broad diffraction peaks at ∼ 23.0°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphene [27]. This observation suggests that BA is decorated on the edges without intercalating into the graphene layers. The Raman spectrum of NCQDs exhibits prominent peaks at 1367 and 1596 cm−1, associated with the typical D and G bands, respectively, confirming the graphene structure of the synthesized NCQDs [28]. The D and G bands of BA-NCQDs are observed at 1378 and 1581 cm−1, respectively. Compared with the D and G peaks of NCQDs, the slight shifts observed in those of BA-NCQDs confirm electron transfer between NCQDs and BA [29]. Bands observed at 1248, 1296, and 1460 cm−1 correspond to two parallel naphthalene fragments of the perylene-like core of PTCDA (Fig. 1d) [30]. The I D/I G ratio for BA-NCQDs is 0.87, slightly higher than that for NCQDs (0.85), indicating an increase in structural defects in BA-NCQDs upon conjugation of NCQDs with BA.

The FTIR spectra of NCQDs and BA-NCQDs are similar owing to their similar functional groups, showing peaks at 3433, 1642, 1461, 1380, and 1163 cm−1, ascribed to the vibration of O–H/N–H, NH–C = O, C = C, C = C, and C–O, respectively (Fig. S1a). However, BA-NCQDs exhibit a new peak at 1550 cm−1, resulting from the formation of another amide bond between NCQDs and BA [31]. The UV–vis spectrum of NCQDs (Fig. S1b) shows two absorption peaks at 489 and 524 nm, corresponding to the π–π* transition of the macrocyclic aromatic conjugated structure and the n–π* transition of the C = O group, respectively. The absorption peaks observed at 218 and 259 nm are associated with the π − π* transition of the C = C bond and n–π* transition of the C–N group, respectively. After linking with BA, the aforementioned four peaks blue-shifted to 246, 325, 496, and 533 nm, predominantly owing to the conjugation with BA [32].

XPS characterization validated the chemical compositions and valence states of NCQDs and BA-NCQDs. As illustrated in Fig. S2a, the peaks of XPS survey situated at approximately 284.9, 400.2, and 531.6 eV correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively [33,34]. In contrast to NCQDs, BA-NCQDs exhibit an additional peak at 70.7 eV, ascribed to Br3d. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of NCQDs displays four peaks at 283.97, 284.32, 284.62, and 287.37 eV, indicating the presence of C–C/C = C, C–N, C–O, and N–C = O groups, respectively (Fig. S2b). The N1s spectrum for NCQDs reveals the existence of –NH–C = O, C–N–C, and N–H groups at 398.22, 398.87, and 400.72 eV, respectively (Fig. S2c). The high-resolution C1s spectrum for BA-NCQDs comprises five peaks at 284.23, 284.63, 285.58, 286.13, and 287.48 eV, representing C–C/C = C, C–N, C–Br, –NH–C = O, and C = N groups, respectively (Fig. S2d). The N1s spectrum for BA-NCQDs features three peaks at 398.83, 399.28, and 400.98 eV, corresponding to –NH–C = O, C–NH–C, and C = N groups, respectively (Fig. S2e). Moreover, the Br3d peak is observed at 70.03 eV (Fig. S2f) [35]. Collectively, these findings indicate the successful preparation of both NCQDs and BA-NCQDs.

Mass spectrometry utilizing the matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization time-of-flight technique was employed to further delineate the possible molecular structures [36–38]. As depicted in Fig. S3a, the mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) value for NCQDs (M1) was identified at 739.38. After the removal of two –NH2 groups, an m/z value of 709.2 was observed. The m/z value for BA-NCQDs was determined to be 973.2 (Fig. S3b). Upon the loss of one –OH group and the incorporation of one H+, an m/z value of 957.0 was detected for BA-NCQDs (M2). The decomposition of BA-NCQDs revealed m/z values of 939.6 and 915.7, corresponding to the species [M2-2OH + H]+ and [M2-C(O)NH2-NH + H]+, respectively. A comprehensive summary of the removal and addition of various species is provided in Table S1. The proposed molecular structures for the NCQDs and BA-NCQDs are presented in Fig. S3c and 3d, respectively.

Upon excitation at 500 nm, the NCQDs exhibited an emission peak at 552 nm (Fig. S1c). However, the fluorescence of BA-NCQDs red-shifted to 574 nm, attributed to the reduction of edge defects and the incorporation of –Br [38]. The addition of KYNA led to a noticeable decline in the fluorescence intensity of BA-NCQDs (Fig. 2 a). The fluorescence quenching efficiency of BA-NCQDs was directly proportional to the KYNA concentration within the range of 8.3–81.0 μM (Fig. 2b). The derived linear regression equation was (F 0 − F)/F 0 = 0.0062 × C KYNA + 0.018, delivering a correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.998. The limit of detection (LOD) for KYNA was computed to be 0.05 µM (LOD = 3σ/slope), which is considerably lower than the previously reported fluorescence sensor LOD of 0.02 mM [39]. The UV–vis absorption peaks of BA-NCQDs at 325 nm were markedly enhanced upon the addition of different concentrations of KYNA, suggesting the occurrence of electron transfer (Fig. 2c). The linear regression equation was Y abs = 0.0038 × C KYNA + 0.62, with an R 2 of 0.99 (Fig. 2d). The LOD obtained from the UV–vis spectra was 0.37 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular mechanism of BA-NCQDs response to KYNA
ce:section.ce:para: According to a previous report [17], BA emits fluorescence emission peak at 472 nm. Herein, excitation occurred at 500 nm. Thus, fluorescence predominantly originated from the NCQDs moiety, as depicted in Fig. S1c. NCQDs exhibited maximum fluorescence at 552 nm, which red-shifted to 574 nm after decorating with BA. The redshift of the fluorescence is attributed to increased structural defects in NCQDs and enhanced electron delocalization, as observed in the Raman and UV–vis spectra. An amide formed and linked the NCQDs with BA, creating a typical PET sensor [40] comprising a fluorophore (NCQDs), receptor (BA), and linker (the amide). The linker is usually an atom (e.g., oxygen and nitrogen) or a short functional group (e.g., amide) [41]. When the analyte binds to the sensor based on the PET mechanism, it enhances or blocks electron transfer within the sensor molecule, resulting in fluorescence quenching or enhancement.

To understand the fluorescent sensing mechanism, we investigated the temperature-induced effects on the fluorescence intensity of BA-NCQDs after adding various KYNA concentrations. The quenching process was assessed through the Stern–Volmer equation, expressed as F 0/F = 1 + K sv [Q] [42,43], where K sv symbolizes the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] represents the KYNA concentration. Here, F 0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity of BA-NCQDs without and with KYNA, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2e, the slope of the calibration plot represented K sv, evaluated to be 8.67 × 103, 6.89 × 103, and 5.18 × 103 L/mol at temperatures of 298.15, 308.15, and 318.15 K, with correlation coefficients of 0.998, 0.999, and 0.998, respectively. The decreasing K sv values with increasing temperature from 298.15 to 318.15 K confirm a static quenching mechanism, indicating the formation of a complex between KYNA and BA-NCQDs. In the presence of KYNA, the fluorescence lifetime of BA-NCQDs decreased from 5.14 to 5.03 ns (Fig. 2f), and the fluorescence intensity of BA-NCQDs notably diminished. These findings validate the ultrafast electron-transfer interaction between BA-NCQDs and KYNA [48].

The molecular mechanism was computationally investigated to understand the interaction between BA-NCQDs and KYNA. Three interaction positions were considered based on the molecular structure of BA-NCQDs: (a) KYNA near the linker amide, (b) KYNA near the amide of the NCQDs moiety, and (c) KYNA near the bromine atom. The optimized structures and energies of the BA-NCQDs/KYNA complexes are described in Fig. 3 and Table S2. The calculations showed that the binding energies (E ad) for (a), (b), and (c) models are − 9.9334, −1.1207, and − 53.6615 kcal/mol, respectively. This indicates that the configuration of KYNA binding to the liker amide (Fig. 3a) is more stable compared to the Fig. 3b and 3c. The BA-NCQDs/KYNA complex mainly forms through two considerably short hydrogen bonds: (=O⋯H–O–BA, 2.722 Å) and (BA = O⋯H–N, 1.969 Å), attributed to the interaction between the amide bond on BA-NCQDs and the carboxyl group of KYNA. Such interaction increases the electron cloud density of BA and enhances its electron-donor ability [44]. In BA-NCQDs, NCQDs act as the electron acceptor, while BA serves as the electron donor. The increased number of electron donors strengthens the PET process, leading to fluorescence quenching.

Alternative prevalent biologically related substances, such as saccharides and amino acids, yielded minimal fluorescence response signals (Fig. S4). 3-Hydroxykynurenic acid is one of the metabolites in the kynurenine pathway, and it has a structure similar to KYNA [45]. The selectivity of BA-NCQDs for 3-hydroxyquinolinic acid has been experimentally verified. The results show that BA-NCQDs exhibit a weak response to 3-hydroxyquinolinic acid. Further experiments indicate that the presence of 3-hydroxyquinolinic acid as an interfering substance does not significantly affect the selectivity of BA-NCQDs for KYNA. Kynurenine and N-formykynurenine did not show any interference either. The results revealed that the relative fluorescence intensity of BA-NCQDs remained largely unaltered by other coexisting entities, suggesting that the responsiveness of the system to KYNA surpasses that of other species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spiked-recovery sensing KYNA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the system in biological fluids, BA-NCQDs was employed to detect KYNA in human serum and urine samples. It is well-known that spiked recovery of blank samples is used for the initial establishment and verification of analytical methods [46]. The KYNA is negative in our case in real samples (Fig. S5). As depicted in Fig. S6a, the fluorescence intensity of the BA-NCQDs solution combined with human serum declined considerably upon the introduction of specific KYNA quantities. The relative fluorescence intensity exhibited a compelling linear correlation with KYNA concentrations spanning from 8.3 to 77.0 μM, with the linear regression equation determined as (F 0 − F)/F 0 = 0.0075 × C KYNA + 0.021 and a correlation coefficient of 0.998 (Fig. S6b). Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of BA-NCQDs in human urine also decreased considerably with varying KYNA concentrations (Fig. S7a). The corresponding linear regression equation was (F 0 − F)/F 0 = 0.0074 × C KYNA + 0.019, resulting in a correlation coefficient of 0.99 (Fig. S7b). The resulting recovery outcomes are presented in Table S3, showing percentages ranging from 97.7 % to 101.6 %, with relative standard deviations between 0.5 % and 4.2 %. These findings suggest that BA-NCQDs is viable for KYNA detection in authentic samples.

The cytotoxicity of BA-NCQDs was assessed using the CCK8 assay. As illustrated in Fig. S8, cytotoxicity was measured based on varying incubation durations and concentrations. The cell survival rate was 88 % at a concentration of 50 µg/mL after 72 h of incubation. For incubation periods of 72 h at concentrations of 75 and 100 µg/mL, the cell survival rates were greater than 91 % and 87 %, respectively. A visual assessment of living and deceased ARPE-19 cells was conducted using Calcein-AM and PI solution following incubation with BA-NCQDs (Fig. S9). Living cells emitted a green fluorescence signal, whereas deceased cells exhibited a red fluorescence signal. Even after 72 h of incubation at 100 µg/mL, imaging data revealed only occasional red fluorescent cells. The in vivo toxicity of BA-NCQDs was studied by dissecting major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) from mice treated with BA-NCQDs (Fig. S10, 200 μL, 10 mg/kg). No obvious inflammation, cell necrosis, or apoptosis was observed in the analyzed tissue slices, indicating that BA-NCQDs poses no side effects and demonstrates good biocompatibility.

The practical applicability of the BA-NCQDs sensing system was further evaluated for imaging KYNA within biological systems. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 a, 4d, 4 g, and 4j, the ARPE-19 cell framework was stained red by rhodamine–phalloidin. The overlay images of the cells are displayed in Fig. 4c, 4f, 4i, and 4 l. Cells treated with BA-NCQDs displayed vivid green fluorescence (Fig. 4b). Nonetheless, upon the introduction of KYNA into the ARPE-19 cell culture medium, a distinct reduction in the fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. 4e, 4 h, and 4 k). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of cells treated with BA-NCQDs proportionally diminished upon the addition of varying KYNA concentrations (Fig. 4v).

The biological role of KYNA in food including of onions primarily involves its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, neuroprotective, and immunomodulatory potential, which underscores its significant research value in health promotion [47]. Its content in food varies greatly, ranging from trace levels to as much as 2 mg per gram in chestnut honey and onion [48]. Although KYNA is present in onion tissue, its concentration is relatively low, and direct detection of trace amounts may face challenge of insufficient sensitivity. Therefore, we subjected the onion to KYNA solutions, and tried to image the uptake of KYNA by onion tissues. In the biological experiments, onion tissues were first incubated with BA-NCQDs for 2 h in a 24-well culture plate. Observations revealed that these onion tissues emitted a green fluorescence signal (Fig. S11b). Subsequent treatment with different concentrations of KYNA for an additional 0.5 h led to a noticeable decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S11e and 11 h). Bright-field images are depicted in Fig. S11a, 11d, and 11 g. Overlay images are depicted in Fig. S11c, 11f, and 11i. These findings suggest that the BA-NCQDs fluorescence sensing system possesses tissue penetration capabilities and can be effectively employed for KYNA imaging in biological tissues.

Herein, highly water-dispersible BA-NCQDs were synthesized to function as a fluorescence sensing system for KYNA detection in aqueous media. The addition of KYNA notably reduced the fluorescence intensity of BA-NCQDs, ascribed to a static quenching mechanism. This change in the fluorescence intensity reflects the strength of the PET molecular effect. The interaction of KYNA with the amide linker in BA-NCQDs facilitated electron transfer from BA to NCQDs, thereby inducing a PET process. The LOD of KYNA was calculated to be 0.05 μM. Notably, BA-NCQDs were successfully employed to image KYNA in living human cells and onion tissues. Additionally, recovery experiments results highlighted the efficiency of this sensing system for detecting KYNA in human serum and urine, with a linear range of 8.3–77 μM. This research offers a promising alternative approach for clinical diagnosis of metabolism-related diseases.

Rumei Cheng: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yuying Fang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhixuan Hu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation. Hui Li: Visualization, Validation. Juncheng Li: Validation, Formal analysis. Kaihui Nan: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Yune Zhao: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Shengju Ou: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405115, 82371042), the Wenzhou Bureau of Science and Technology (Y20210081), Project of State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Vision Science of Wenzhou Medical University (J02-20210202), “Pioneer” and “Leading Goose” R&D Program of Zhejiang (2022C03070), the Provincial Construction Project of Zhejiang, (KJ-ZJ-2135) are acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112524.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
